enrichment.short_description: Genea.app is a free, open-source, serverless browser tool for building and editing GEDCOM-compatible family trees, with optional Git-based syncing across devices.
enrichment.seo_title: Genea.app Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Genea.app is a free, open-source, browser-based family tree builder with GEDCOM support and Git syncing. See features, pricing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Genea.app is a free, open-source, browser-based tool for building, editing, and visualizing family trees using the GEDCOM genealogy data standard. The project is maintained under the genea-app organization on GitHub, with its source code released under the MIT License and a copyright date of 2021, which places the project's origin around that year. Unlike most consumer genealogy products, Genea.app does not require an account, a subscription, or a backend server of any kind. It is described by its creators as a 'completely serverless solution for consuming and authoring GEDCOM genealogy data,' meaning every part of the experience, from parsing an imported file to rendering the interactive tree, happens entirely inside the user's web browser. Because there is no server processing personal data, Genea.app markets itself explicitly around 'privacy by design': family history data, which can include sensitive information about living relatives, never leaves the user's device unless the user chooses to export or sync it themselves. This positions the tool as an alternative to hosted genealogy platforms such as Ancestry, MyHeritage, or FamilySearch, which store family tree data on their own servers and often gate advanced features behind paid membership tiers. Genea.app's core workflow starts either from a blank tree or from an imported GEDCOM file, the open, decades-old interchange format supported by virtually every genealogy application, including the major commercial services. Because Genea.app both reads and writes standard GEDCOM, a user can bring in a tree exported from MyHeritage, FamilySearch, or Ancestry, edit and extend it locally, and then export it again or move it to a different tool entirely, avoiding vendor lock-in. To help new users understand the interface before committing their own family data, the site also ships with a handful of sample trees, including a Royal lineage, a set of past United States presidents, and a fictional Harry Potter family tree, that visitors can explore immediately without importing anything. Since Genea.app keeps no server-side copy of a user's tree, the project had to solve the multi-device synchronization problem differently than a typical SaaS app. Its answer is twofold. First, users can simply save their GEDCOM file locally and move it between devices manually, for example by emailing it to themselves or storing it in a general-purpose cloud drive such as Dropbox. Second, and more distinctively, Genea.app includes a built-in Git integration, letting users connect the app to a private or public Git repository, whether hosted on GitHub, GitLab, or a self-hosted Git server such as Gitea, Gogs, or GitBucket, and use ordinary version-controlled commits to store and sync tree changes. This Git-based approach gives technically inclined users full version history of their genealogical research, the ability to branch and experiment with alternative theories about ancestry, and complete control over where their data physically lives, all without Genea.app itself hosting or ever seeing that data. The tool works on both desktop and mobile browsers, so a user can review or lightly edit a tree from a phone as easily as from a laptop, though heavier editing sessions are more practical on larger screens given the visual, node-based nature of family tree interfaces. Because Genea.app is a small, community-maintained open-source project rather than a funded company, it does not publish information about company size, headquarters, revenue, or a named founder; contributions and issue discussion happen publicly through the genea-app GitHub organization, and the project's roadmap and support channel are the GitHub issue tracker itself. This also means Genea.app has no formal customer support team, no enterprise sales process, and no roadmap commitments beyond what volunteer maintainers choose to build. Its closest competitors fall into two very different categories: large hosted genealogy platforms like Ancestry, MyHeritage, FamilySearch, and Geni, which offer research databases, DNA matching, and record hints but require accounts and often subscriptions; and other open-source or self-hosted family tree tools such as Gramps (a mature, feature-rich desktop application) and GeneWeb (a server-based genealogy engine, also covered separately in this directory), which target more advanced genealogists willing to install and run software rather than use a purely in-browser tool. Genea.app's differentiation is its combination of zero installation, zero account requirement, and zero server dependency, which makes it attractive to privacy-conscious hobbyists, developers who want to inspect or extend an open codebase, and anyone who wants a lightweight way to view or lightly edit a GEDCOM file without committing to a larger genealogy ecosystem. The tradeoff is that it lacks the record-hint databases, DNA integration, and collaborative research communities that make paid platforms valuable for serious ancestry research; Genea.app is best understood as a focused, no-frills tree editor and viewer rather than a full genealogy research service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Genea.app is a free, open-source, browser-based application for creating and editing family trees using the GEDCOM standard. It runs entirely client-side, with no account, no server, and no backend database, which the project frames as a privacy-first alternative to hosted genealogy platforms.

The tool is maintained on GitHub under the MIT License by the genea-app organization. It is designed for people who already have, or want to create, a standard GEDCOM file and prefer to keep full control of where that data is stored rather than uploading it to a commercial genealogy service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users can start a tree from scratch or import an existing GEDCOM file exported from services like MyHeritage, FamilySearch, or Ancestry, then edit and re-export it at any time, avoiding vendor lock-in.

For multi-device use, Genea.app offers manual file export/import as well as a built-in Git integration that syncs tree changes to GitHub, GitLab, or self-hosted Git servers such as Gitea, Gogs, or GitBucket, giving users full version history of their research.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Genea.app is completely free and open source; there are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or account requirements of any kind.

Because the project has no hosting costs tied to user data (all processing happens in the browser), there is no usage-based billing model either, making it effectively free for unlimited personal use.

features.title: Serverless, browser-based editing
features.description: All tree building and editing happens locally in the browser, so no personal genealogy data is transmitted to or stored on Genea.app servers.

features.title: GEDCOM import and export
features.description: Fully compatible with the GEDCOM standard, allowing trees to be imported from and exported to Ancestry, MyHeritage, FamilySearch, and other genealogy tools.

features.title: Git-based sync and version history
features.description: Connects to GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, Gogs, or GitBucket so users can commit tree changes and keep a full version history of their research.

features.title: No account required
features.description: Users can start building a tree immediately without signing up, entering an email address, or creating a password.

features.title: Sample trees for exploration
features.description: Preloaded example trees, including royal lineages, US presidents, and a Harry Potter family tree, let new users learn the interface risk-free.

features.title: Cross-device compatibility
features.description: Works in modern browsers on both desktop and mobile devices for viewing and light editing on the go.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Released under the MIT License on GitHub, allowing developers to inspect, self-host, fork, or extend the application.

features.title: No vendor lock-in
features.description: Because data is stored as a standard GEDCOM file that the user controls, switching to another genealogy tool later requires no migration process.

pros: Completely free with no paid tiers or hidden costs
Strong privacy model since no genealogy data is sent to a remote server
Open-source and self-inspectable, appealing to technical and privacy-conscious users
Git integration gives version-controlled history of family tree research
No account or sign-up friction to start building a tree
cons: Lacks the record-hint databases, historical archives, and DNA matching found in paid genealogy platforms
No built-in collaborative research community or shared family tree hosting
Git-based syncing has a learning curve for non-technical users
As a small volunteer-maintained project, there is no dedicated customer support or guaranteed roadmap
Editing large, complex trees is more comfortable on desktop than mobile

faqs.question: Is Genea.app really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Genea.app is free and open source with no subscription plans, paid tiers, or account requirements.

faqs.question: Does Genea.app store my family tree data on its servers?
faqs.answer: No. All processing happens in the browser, so Genea.app does not host or store user genealogy data; users choose where their GEDCOM file is saved.

faqs.question: Can I import a tree from Ancestry or MyHeritage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Genea.app supports the standard GEDCOM format, which is exportable from Ancestry, MyHeritage, FamilySearch, and most other genealogy services.

faqs.question: How do I sync my tree across multiple devices?
faqs.answer: You can manually export and move the GEDCOM file yourself, or use Genea.app's built-in Git integration to sync changes through GitHub, GitLab, or a self-hosted Git server.

faqs.question: Is Genea.app open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is released under the MIT License and maintained publicly on GitHub under the genea-app organization.

faqs.question: Does Genea.app offer DNA matching or historical record search?
faqs.answer: No, Genea.app is focused purely on building and editing GEDCOM family trees; it does not include DNA matching or record-hint research features found on commercial platforms.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious family tree editing
use_cases.description: Users who don't want their family history data hosted on a third-party server can build and edit trees entirely offline in the browser.

use_cases.title: Portable GEDCOM editing between services
use_cases.description: Import a GEDCOM file exported from a commercial platform, make edits or corrections, then re-export it without being locked into one vendor.

use_cases.title: Version-controlled family research
use_cases.description: Technical users can track the evolution of their family tree over time using Git commits, branches, and history.

features: Unlimited family tree creation and editing
Git-based sync (GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, Gogs, GitBucket)

enrichment.short_description: Genealogy is a free, open-source, Laravel-built web application for recording family members, relationships, and multi-tree family history research.
enrichment.seo_title: Genealogy (kreaweb) Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Genealogy is a free, open-source Laravel family tree app for recording relationships and ancestry. See its features, licensing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Genealogy, hosted at genealogy.kreaweb.be, is a free and open-source family tree web application built with the Laravel PHP framework. It is developed and maintained by KREAWEB, a web development studio credited on GitHub under the account MGeurts, with the underlying code published as an MIT-licensed repository named 'genealogy'. Public documentation about the project describes it as evolving alongside successive Laravel releases, with KREAWEB's own blog referencing a 'Laravel 11 - Genealogy' build and the GitHub repository description referencing Laravel 13, indicating the project is actively kept current with the Laravel ecosystem rather than being a static, abandoned codebase. Unlike large commercial genealogy platforms that charge subscriptions for access to historical records and DNA matching, Genealogy is explicitly positioned as free to use for non-commercial purposes, targeting individual family history researchers and hobbyists rather than professional genealogists or commercial data brokers. The application's core feature set centers on structured family record-keeping: users create accounts, then build out family trees by recording individuals, their partnerships (including explicit support for same-sex couples), and their children, establishing a relational family structure that can be browsed and explored. A distinguishing organizational feature is its support for teams, where a family tree can be owned by a team of collaborators rather than a single user, and different teams can be created for different family trees, which is useful for research projects that involve multiple contributing relatives or collaborators working on the same lineage. To help prospective users understand the tool without creating an account or entering real family data, the live site includes two public demonstration family trees: one modeling the British Royal Family and another modeling the Kennedy family, both well-documented public lineages that make it easy to see how the tool renders multi-generational relationships, marriages, and children in practice. On the data portability front, the project has been actively building GEDCOM import and export capability, the same open interchange standard used across the genealogy software industry (including by Ancestry, MyHeritage, and FamilySearch), with initial import/export capabilities already functional and further development ongoing at the time of research; this signals an intent to let users move family tree data in and out of the platform rather than lock it in permanently. Because Genealogy is a self-hosted-friendly, open-source Laravel application rather than a funded startup product, there is no publicly disclosed founding year, employee count, or funding history in the way there would be for a venture-backed company; what public information exists points to KREAWEB, credited in project materials as based in New York, NY, as the studio behind its design and development, with the GitHub user MGeurts serving as the primary listed developer and maintainer. The MIT license under which the code is distributed means any developer can inspect, self-host, fork, or extend the application for their own use, which is a meaningful differentiator from closed, proprietary genealogy SaaS platforms. In terms of competitive positioning, Genealogy competes less with giants like Ancestry or MyHeritage, which differentiate primarily through massive proprietary historical record archives and DNA testing services, and more with other lightweight, self-hostable or free genealogy tools such as Gramps, GeneWeb, and Genea.app (also covered separately in this directory), all of which appeal to users who want direct control over their family data without a subscription. Genealogy's particular niche within that smaller open-source category is its modern web-app UX built on Laravel, its explicit multi-user 'team' collaboration model for shared family trees, and its support for inclusive family structures such as same-sex couples, which is not always a given in older or more traditionally structured genealogy software. Because the software's language support spans at least eleven languages according to the project's own materials, it is clearly designed with an international, non-English-first user base in mind, which is somewhat unusual for a small independently maintained open-source project and suggests either community-contributed translations or deliberate internationalization work by the KREAWEB team. Overall, Genealogy is best understood as a modern, actively developed, free, and mission-driven alternative to paid genealogy platforms for users who want a straightforward, web-based way to record and browse family relationships without financial cost or vendor lock-in, at the tradeoff of not offering the historical record databases, DNA matching, or large-scale hint systems that define the commercial genealogy category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Genealogy is a free, open-source family tree application built on the Laravel PHP framework and maintained by web studio KREAWEB (GitHub user MGeurts). It focuses on recording individuals, partnerships, and children to build browsable, multi-generational family trees.

The project is distributed under the MIT License, is explicitly free for non-commercial use, and supports at least eleven languages, reflecting an international, community-oriented approach rather than a commercial SaaS model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality includes recording persons, partners and couples (including same-sex couples), and children, plus team-based ownership so multiple collaborators can work on a shared family tree.

The platform ships with two public demo trees, the British Royal Family and the Kennedy family, and includes GEDCOM import/export functionality that is actively being expanded for broader interoperability with other genealogy tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Genealogy is free to use for non-commercial purposes, with no paid subscription tiers currently documented.
Because it is open source under the MIT License, technically inclined users can also self-host their own instance of the application at no licensing cost.

features.title: Family tree building
features.description: Record individuals, partnerships, couples (including same-sex couples), and children to construct multi-generational family trees.

features.title: Team-based collaboration
features.description: Create separate teams for each family tree and invite collaborators to research and edit together.

features.description: Actively developed support for the GEDCOM standard, allowing data exchange with other genealogy platforms.

features.description: Built-in demonstration trees for the British Royal Family and the Kennedy family let new users explore the tool without entering personal data.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: The interface supports at least eleven languages, including English, French, Spanish, German, and Mandarin.

features.title: Open-source, self-hostable codebase
features.description: Released under the MIT License and built on Laravel, allowing developers to inspect, modify, or self-host the application.

features.title: Account-based access
features.description: User registration and login system to keep personal family tree data private to the owning team.

Open source under a permissive MIT License
Team-based collaboration model for shared family research
Inclusive support for same-sex couples in family structures
Broad multi-language support for an international audience
cons: No public historical record archive or DNA matching, unlike commercial genealogy platforms
GEDCOM import/export functionality is still being actively developed and expanded
No published company history, funding, or long-term roadmap since it is a small studio project
Smaller user community than established players like Ancestry or MyHeritage
Founding year and company scale are not publicly documented

faqs.question: Is Genealogy free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free to use for non-commercial purposes.

faqs.question: Who built Genealogy?
faqs.answer: It is developed and maintained by KREAWEB, a web development studio, with GitHub user MGeurts credited as the primary developer.

faqs.question: What technology is Genealogy built on?
faqs.answer: It is a PHP web application built with the Laravel framework, with the codebase publicly available and updated alongside newer Laravel releases.

faqs.question: Can I import my existing family tree?
faqs.answer: GEDCOM import and export functionality is available and under active development, allowing data exchange with other genealogy tools.

faqs.question: Does Genealogy support same-sex couples in family trees?
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform explicitly supports recording partners and couples, including same-sex couples.

faqs.question: Is the source code available?
faqs.answer: Yes, Genealogy is released under the MIT License and its source is publicly available on GitHub.

enrichment.short_description: General Translation is a developer-focused localization and internationalization platform that combines an AI translation agent, SDKs for React and Next.js, a translation editor, and a global translation CDN.
languages: and 100+ supported target languages for translation
enrichment.seo_title: General Translation Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: General Translation is a developer i18n and AI localization platform with SDKs, a translation CDN, and an AI agent. Compare features, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: General Translation, built by General Translation, Inc. and marketed at generaltranslation.com, is a full-stack internationalization and localization platform aimed squarely at software developers who need to ship multilingual products without building translation infrastructure from scratch. The company was founded in 2023 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with Archie McKenzie serving as founder and CEO; McKenzie has also been recognized as a member of the Forbes Technology Council, reflecting the company's visibility in the developer-tools space. According to startup-tracking sources, General Translation has raised early-stage funding reported in the range of roughly $2.4 to $2.7 million, though exact, audited figures are not publicly confirmed, and the company has not disclosed a specific funding round name or lead investor in publicly available materials. Rather than positioning itself as a traditional translation vendor that simply converts text from one language to another, General Translation frames its product as an end-to-end localization stack covering the entire developer workflow, from detecting untranslated strings in a codebase to serving translated content in production with low latency. The platform's centerpiece is 'Locadex,' an AI agent that connects directly to a development team's codebase, automatically identifies and internationalizes hard-coded UI text, and opens pull requests containing the necessary code changes and translations, triggered either manually or automatically by new commits and pull requests. This agent-driven approach differentiates General Translation from older-generation i18n libraries that require developers to manually wrap every string in a translation function and manage translation files by hand. Around this AI agent, the company has built a set of developer SDKs supporting React, Next.js, React Native, Node.js, and Python, giving engineering teams framework-native components for handling translated UI text, pluralization, currency and date formatting, and locale-based routing, which is particularly relevant for Next.js applications that need SEO-friendly, per-locale URL structures. For teams that want human oversight of AI-generated translations, General Translation provides a Translation Editor, a review workspace where linguists or bilingual team members can see source and translated text side by side, track diffs between versions, and approve or edit machine translations before they ship, addressing a common concern that purely automated translation pipelines can introduce embarrassing or contextually wrong phrasing. To actually deliver translated content to end users, the platform operates a Translation CDN, a globally distributed content delivery layer that can push over-the-air translation updates to a live application without requiring a full redeployment, which shortens the feedback loop for fixing or improving translations after launch. The product also supports live translation of user-generated content, automatic language detection, and configurable context controls such as glossaries and locale-specific rules, letting teams enforce consistent terminology (for example, keeping brand names untranslated) across all supported languages, which the company states now number more than 100 languages and locales. On pricing, General Translation follows a freemium model: the entry-level 'Starter' plan costs $0 and includes unlimited users, unlimited projects, and unlimited languages, with usage-based charges kicking in via a per-workflow pricing model once a team exceeds included token or workflow limits; billing is based specifically on the length of input content submitted for translation, not on the length of the resulting translation output, which the company highlights as a fairer approach than input-plus-output billing used by some competitors. Teams with more complex needs can move to a custom 'Enterprise' plan that adds custom integrations, a custom SLA, and forward-deployed engineering support, priced individually per contract. The company also emphasizes enterprise-readiness through security and compliance credentials including SOC 2 Type II, GDPR compliance, and ISO 27001, positioning General Translation as usable by security-conscious teams at larger organizations, not just early-stage startups. Its publicly referenced customers and integration partners include recognizable developer-tooling and AI-adjacent brands such as Cursor, Ramp, Mintlify, Profound, Partiful, and ClickHouse, suggesting traction among fast-growing technical companies that need to localize product interfaces or documentation quickly. In the competitive landscape, General Translation sits alongside established localization platforms such as Lokalise, Crowdin, Phrase, and Weblate, as well as raw machine-translation APIs from Google Cloud Translation, DeepL, and Microsoft Translator. Its differentiation is the combination of an agentic AI layer that automates the internationalization step itself (not just the translation step), tight framework-specific SDKs for the modern React and Next.js ecosystem, and a CDN-based delivery model built for continuous, low-friction translation updates, rather than a generic translation-management-system workflow bolted onto an existing product. This makes General Translation particularly attractive to product and engineering teams at startups and mid-sized companies building React or Next.js applications who want to add multilingual support quickly without hiring a dedicated localization team, while larger enterprises with existing localization operations and compliance requirements are served through its custom Enterprise tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: General Translation, founded in 2023 by CEO Archie McKenzie and headquartered in San Francisco, is a developer-first localization platform combining an AI internationalization agent, framework-native SDKs, a human translation review editor, and a global translation content delivery network.

The company positions itself around automating not just translation but the underlying internationalization work of a codebase, using its 'Locadex' AI agent to find and wrap untranslated strings and open pull requests automatically.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform supports React, Next.js, React Native, Node.js, and Python through dedicated SDKs, and covers over 100 languages and locales with automatic language detection and locale-aware routing.

A Translation Editor lets human reviewers approve or refine AI-generated translations side by side with source text, while the Translation CDN delivers over-the-air translation updates to live apps without a full redeploy.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: General Translation uses a freemium model. The Starter plan is $0 and includes unlimited users, projects, and languages, with usage-based charges applying per-workflow once included limits are exceeded, billed only on input length rather than translated output length.
The Enterprise plan is custom-priced and adds custom integrations, a dedicated SLA, and forward-deployed engineering support for larger organizations with complex localization and compliance needs.

features.description: Connects to a codebase, automatically detects and internationalizes hard-coded UI text, and opens pull requests triggered by commits or PRs.

features.description: A review workspace where teams can see source and translated text side by side, track diffs, and approve or edit AI-generated translations.

features.title: Global Translation CDN
features.description: A globally distributed delivery layer that pushes over-the-air translation updates to production apps without a full redeploy.

features.title: 100+ language support
features.description: Supports translation into more than 100 languages and locales with automatic language detection.

features.title: Live user-generated content translation
features.description: Translates dynamic, user-generated content in real time, not just static UI strings.

features.title: Glossaries and locale rules
features.description: Custom context controls let teams enforce consistent terminology, such as keeping brand names untranslated, across all languages.

features.title: Enterprise compliance
features.description: SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, and ISO 27001 compliance support security-conscious enterprise adoption.

pros: Free Starter tier with unlimited users, projects, and languages
Automates internationalization work, not just translation, via the Locadex AI agent
Deep framework-native support for React and Next.js, including SEO-friendly locale routing
Billed only on input length, not translated output length
Enterprise-grade compliance certifications (SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, ISO 27001)
cons: Enterprise pricing is fully custom and not published, requiring a sales conversation
AI-driven translation still benefits from human review for nuanced or brand-sensitive content
Best suited to React/Next.js-centric stacks; other frameworks have less native tooling
As a relatively young company founded in 2023, long-term track record and stability are still being established
Exact funding figures are not officially disclosed, only reported by third-party startup trackers

faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter plan is free ($0) and includes unlimited users, projects, and languages, with usage-based charges applying only once included workflow limits are exceeded.

faqs.question: What frameworks does General Translation support?
faqs.answer: It offers dedicated SDKs for React, Next.js, React Native, Node.js, and Python.

faqs.answer: Locadex is General Translation's AI agent that connects to a codebase, automatically internationalizes hard-coded UI text, and opens pull requests with the changes and translations.

faqs.question: How many languages does General Translation support?
faqs.answer: The platform supports translation into more than 100 languages and locales.

faqs.answer: Yes. It offers a custom Enterprise plan with custom integrations, an SLA, forward-deployed engineers, and supports SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, and ISO 27001 compliance.

faqs.question: Who founded General Translation?
faqs.answer: General Translation was founded in 2023 by CEO Archie McKenzie and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

enrichment.short_description: Geneva is a free, all-in-one group communication app combining chat, audio rooms, livestreaming, forums, and event planning for clubs and communities, now owned by Bumble Inc.
enrichment.seo_title: Geneva Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Geneva is a free group chat and community app with rooms, livestreaming, and event planning. See features, pricing, and top Geneva alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Geneva is a free, all-in-one communication app designed for groups, clubs, and communities rather than enterprise workplace teams, positioning itself with the tagline 'the online place to find your offline people.' The company was founded in 2018 by Justin Hauser, who is also known as a co-founder of the CBD beverage company Recess, and is headquartered in New York City. Over its lifetime as an independent company, Geneva raised outside funding reported at roughly $26.5 million across three rounds from four investors, according to Crunchbase-style aggregator data, before reaching a significant milestone in its corporate history: the company was acquired by Bumble Inc., the publicly traded parent company of the Bumble dating app, in a deal that closed on July 1, 2024. Following the acquisition, Geneva has continued to operate as a distinct product and brand under Bumble's ownership rather than being immediately folded into or discontinued in favor of Bumble's core dating product, and Geneva's own marketing has publicly celebrated becoming part of Bumble Inc. Functionally, Geneva differentiates itself from both traditional group chat apps like Discord and workplace tools like Slack by focusing on real-world, local community building: the product's core use case is helping people who already share an interest, whether a book club, a running group, a professional network, or a hobbyist community, coordinate both online conversation and offline, in-person meetups and events. The app's feature set is built around multiple distinct 'room' types that a group can mix and match depending on its needs. Chat rooms provide full-featured text messaging with @mentions, threaded replies, emoji reactions, GIFs, file attachments, polls, message pinning, and typing indicators, covering the baseline expectations of a modern group chat product. Audio rooms let members hang out in live voice conversations, similar in spirit to Discord voice channels or Clubhouse-style audio spaces. Forum rooms support longer-form, threaded discussion better suited to in-depth topics than a fast-scrolling chat feed. Broadcast rooms allow a group to livestream events, tutorials, or gatherings such as virtual dance parties to an audience, with up to nine people able to appear live on-air simultaneously while thousands of viewers can tune in to watch, giving Geneva a lightweight built-in live video broadcasting capability. Video rooms round out the real-time communication options, supporting group video calls with up to sixteen simultaneous participants. On top of these communication surfaces, Geneva includes organizational tooling aimed specifically at community management: group administrators can screen prospective members using custom questionnaires before granting access, plan events with built-in invitation flows and a centralized shared calendar, assign customizable roles and permissions to different members, and, notably, support token-gating, a feature that restricts access to a community based on ownership of a specific blockchain token or NFT, which reflects a period during Geneva's development when web3 and token-gated communities were a notable growth category for consumer social apps. Geneva is available as a free app across iPhone, Android, and the web, with no disclosed premium subscription tier for end users in current public materials, which is consistent with a consumer social product monetized more likely through data, partnerships, or its parent company's broader business model post-acquisition rather than direct subscription revenue from community members. The platform has also attracted attention beyond hobbyist and social communities: trade press coverage has noted that beauty brands have used Geneva alongside Slack for private group messaging with customers and fans, indicating some adoption by consumer brands seeking a more intimate, community-style channel than public social media. In the competitive landscape, Geneva sits between Discord, which dominates gaming and hobbyist communities with similar chat, voice, and now video capabilities but skews toward a younger, gaming-native audience, and platforms like Mighty Networks or Circle, which target creators and course communities with more emphasis on monetization and content libraries than live audio/video interaction. Geneva's specific differentiation is its emphasis on connecting online community activity to real-world, local, in-person events ('the people you want to meet and the things you want to do in your city'), its inclusive positioning toward LGBTQIA+ communities, and its combination of chat, audio, video, forum, and broadcast room types within a single, unified group rather than requiring separate apps or channels for each communication mode. Its acquisition by Bumble Inc. in 2024 also signals a strategic bet by Bumble to expand beyond one-to-one dating matches into broader social and community-based connection, potentially integrating Geneva's group and community infrastructure with Bumble's existing user base and relationship-focused brand over time, though the two products continue to operate under separate branding as of the most recent publicly available information.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Geneva is a free, all-in-one group communication app founded in 2018 by Justin Hauser (also a co-founder of Recess) and headquartered in New York City. It targets clubs, hobby groups, and local communities rather than enterprise teams, combining chat, audio, video, forums, and livestreaming in a single product.

Geneva raised roughly $26.5 million in funding across three rounds before being acquired by Bumble Inc. in a deal that closed on July 1, 2024, and continues to operate as its own brand under Bumble's ownership.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Groups can combine chat rooms, audio rooms, forum rooms, broadcast rooms (livestreaming to up to nine on-air participants and thousands of viewers), and video rooms (up to sixteen participants) within a single community space.
Community management tools include member screening questionnaires, built-in event planning and calendars, customizable roles and permissions, and token-gating for blockchain-based communities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Geneva is free to use on iPhone, Android, and the web, with no publicly disclosed paid subscription tier for community members.
Monetization details post-acquisition by Bumble Inc. are not publicly broken out separately from Bumble's broader corporate financials.

features.title: Multi-format chat rooms
features.description: Full-featured text chat with @mentions, threaded replies, reactions, GIFs, attachments, polls, pins, and typing indicators.

features.title: Audio rooms
features.description: Live voice hangout spaces for group conversation, similar to voice channels or drop-in audio spaces.

features.title: Broadcast/livestream rooms
features.description: Stream events, tutorials, or gatherings live with up to nine people on-air and thousands of viewers watching.

features.title: Video rooms
features.description: Group video calls supporting up to sixteen simultaneous participants.

features.title: Forum rooms
features.description: Threaded, longer-form discussion spaces better suited to in-depth topics than a fast-moving chat feed.

features.title: Event planning and calendar
features.description: Built-in event invitations and a centralized shared calendar to organize both online and offline meetups.

features.title: Member screening and roles
features.description: Custom questionnaires to screen prospective members plus configurable roles and permissions for community management.

features.title: Token-gating
features.description: Restrict community access based on ownership of a specific blockchain token or NFT.

pros: Completely free across iOS, Android, and web
Combines chat, audio, video, forum, and livestreaming in one unified product
Strong focus on turning online communities into real-world, local meetups
Backed by significant funding and now the resources of Bumble Inc. post-acquisition
Inclusive, community-first positioning with LGBTQIA+ focused features
cons: No enterprise-grade workplace features (e.g., admin controls, SSO) compared to tools like Slack
Smaller mainstream user base than Discord for hobbyist and gaming communities
Future product direction is less certain following the 2024 acquisition by Bumble Inc.
No disclosed premium tier or advanced monetization tools for community organizers
Detailed current company size and internal metrics are not publicly disclosed

faqs.question: Is Geneva free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Geneva is free to use on iPhone, Android, and the web.

faqs.question: Who founded Geneva?
faqs.answer: Geneva was founded in 2018 by Justin Hauser, who is also a co-founder of the CBD beverage company Recess.

faqs.question: Is Geneva still an independent company?
faqs.answer: No, Geneva was acquired by Bumble Inc. in a deal that closed on July 1, 2024, and now operates as a Bumble-owned brand.

faqs.question: How is Geneva different from Discord or Slack?
faqs.answer: Geneva focuses on helping local, real-world communities and clubs coordinate both online conversation and in-person events, combining chat, audio, video, forums, and livestreaming in one app, whereas Discord skews toward gaming communities and Slack targets workplace teams.

faqs.question: How many people can join a Geneva video call?
faqs.answer: Geneva video rooms support up to sixteen simultaneous participants.

faqs.question: Does Geneva support token-gated communities?
faqs.answer: Yes, Geneva includes token-gating, which restricts community access based on ownership of a specific blockchain token or NFT.

use_cases.title: Local hobby and interest clubs
use_cases.description: Book clubs, running groups, and other interest-based communities use Geneva to chat online and plan in-person meetups.

use_cases.title: Livestreamed community events

use_cases.title: Brand community engagement
use_cases.description: Consumer brands use Geneva for private group messaging with fans and customers alongside other tools like Slack.

features: Chat, audio, video, forum, and broadcast rooms
Available on iOS, Android, and web

enrichment.short_description: GeneWeb is a free, open-source genealogy software engine written in OCaml, offering a web interface for building, browsing, and analyzing large genealogical databases, used by projects like Roglo.
enrichment.seo_title: GeneWeb Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GeneWeb is a free, open-source OCaml genealogy engine with a web interface. Explore its features, licensing, technical architecture, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GeneWeb is a free, open-source genealogy software engine created by Daniel de Rauglaudre, a researcher at Inria (Institut national de recherche en informatique et en automatique), the French national computer science research institute. GeneWeb was written in OCaml, a functional programming language also developed within Inria's research community, and the project has been active since 1998, making it one of the longest-running open-source genealogy platforms still maintained today. The software is distributed under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPL-2.0), and its source code is maintained on GitHub under the geneweb organization, where the project has accumulated 386 stars and 115 forks at the time of research, with its most recent tracked release being version 7.1.0-beta2. Architecturally, GeneWeb is fundamentally different from typical desktop genealogy software: rather than being a standalone application with its own file browser and print layouts, GeneWeb operates as a lightweight embedded web server that can host and serve any number of genealogical databases simultaneously, dynamically generating HTML pages in response to queries, and viewable through any standard web browser, including Chrome, Firefox, and other modern browsers. This means a single GeneWeb installation can be run entirely offline on a personal computer for private research, or deployed on a remote server (via a background daemon process or through the CGI protocol) to make a family tree browsable over the internet to relatives or the public, which is a major reason the software became the technical foundation for some of the largest publicly accessible genealogical databases in the world. The most prominent example is Roglo, a public genealogy database built on GeneWeb containing more than 8.5 million individuals (with some references citing figures beyond 11 million as the database has continued to grow), demonstrating that the underlying engine can scale to very large, heavily cross-referenced datasets while remaining performant. On the data model and feature side, GeneWeb includes an efficient relationship and consanguinity rate calculator, letting researchers determine how closely related any two people in a database are and compute inbreeding coefficients, a computationally nontrivial feature that reflects the software's academic research origins. It generates a range of built-in statistics automatically from a loaded database, including lists of the oldest living individuals, recently recorded weddings or deaths, and age pyramids for a given year, useful for demographic analysis of large family datasets rather than just individual tree browsing. The software supports rich, wikitext-style formatting in notes attached to individuals and families, including internal cross-references between people and embedded media, giving researchers a way to attach sourced narrative context to bare genealogical facts. For data interchange, GeneWeb supports both its own native 'GW' text-based database format, optimized for its own storage and query engine, and the industry-standard GEDCOM format used by virtually every other genealogy tool, including commercial platforms like Ancestry, MyHeritage, and FamilySearch, meaning researchers can import existing family trees into GeneWeb or export GeneWeb data for use elsewhere. Administration is handled through a dedicated utility called gwsetup, which provides a browser-based interface for configuring and maintaining GeneWeb installations and databases without requiring command-line expertise for routine tasks, lowering the barrier to entry for non-technical genealogists once the software itself has been installed. GeneWeb runs on Unix, Linux, Microsoft Windows, and macOS, and its interface has been localized into at least nine languages (Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Italian, Spanish, and Swedish), reflecting adoption across multiple European genealogy communities beyond its French origin. Within the genealogy software landscape, GeneWeb's closest comparisons are other open-source, self-hosted genealogy engines such as Gramps (a desktop-first application with a plugin ecosystem) and, within this directory, the similarly named but functionally distinct web app Genealogy (a Laravel-based tool) and Genea.app (a serverless, browser-only GEDCOM editor); GeneWeb differs from both by being explicitly designed as a server application capable of hosting very large, multi-million-person public databases with statistical and demographic analysis tools rather than acting as a lightweight personal tree editor. Because GeneWeb has no corporate backing, there is no publicly documented company size, headquarters, or revenue; it exists purely as a community-and-academic-maintained open-source project, with ongoing development now supported by a collaborative group of contributors and translators beyond its original creator, and its long operational history combined with its role powering large-scale public genealogical databases like Roglo represents its primary claim to significance in the genealogy software space.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GeneWeb is a free, open-source genealogy software engine written in OCaml, created in 1998 by Inria researcher Daniel de Rauglaudre. It operates as a lightweight web server capable of hosting any number of genealogical databases and dynamically generating browsable HTML pages.

Released under the GPL-2.0 license and maintained on GitHub, GeneWeb has powered some of the largest public genealogical databases in the world, most notably Roglo, which contains more than 8.5 million individuals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: GeneWeb includes an efficient relationship and consanguinity rate calculator, automatic statistics such as oldest-living-person lists and age pyramids, and wikitext-formatted notes with internal cross-references and embedded media.

It supports both a native GW database format and the industry-standard GEDCOM format for import/export, and includes a browser-based admin utility (gwsetup) for configuring and maintaining installations without command-line expertise.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: GeneWeb is entirely free and open source under the GPL-2.0 license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees.
Users self-host the software on their own hardware or server, so the only ongoing cost is whatever infrastructure the operator chooses to run it on.

features.title: Web-server-based architecture
features.description: Runs as a lightweight embedded web server that hosts and dynamically serves any number of genealogical databases via a standard browser.

features.title: Relationship and consanguinity calculator
features.description: Efficiently computes how closely related two individuals are and calculates inbreeding coefficients within a database.

features.title: Automatic statistics generation
features.description: Produces lists of oldest living individuals, recent weddings or deaths, and age pyramids for a given year.

features.title: GEDCOM and native GW format support
features.description: Imports and exports data in both the industry-standard GEDCOM format and GeneWeb's own native GW format.

features.title: Wikitext notes with cross-references
features.description: Attach richly formatted notes to individuals and families, including internal links and embedded media.

features.title: gwsetup administration tool
features.description: A browser-based configuration and maintenance interface that removes the need for command-line administration.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: Localized into at least nine languages, including French, English, German, Dutch, and Spanish.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs on Unix, Linux, Microsoft Windows, and macOS.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPL-2.0
Proven at massive scale, powering databases like Roglo with millions of individuals
Advanced relationship/consanguinity calculation not commonly found in consumer genealogy tools
Long track record of continuous development since 1998
Flexible deployment as a private offline tool or a public-facing web genealogy database
cons: Requires self-hosting and some technical setup, unlike consumer SaaS genealogy platforms
Web interface design is dated compared to modern commercial genealogy services
No official customer support; relies on community and documentation
No built-in historical record archive or DNA matching like Ancestry or MyHeritage
OCaml-based codebase can be less approachable for contributors unfamiliar with the language

faqs.question: Is GeneWeb free?
faqs.answer: Yes, GeneWeb is free and open source under the GNU General Public License version 2.

faqs.question: Who created GeneWeb?
faqs.answer: GeneWeb was created in 1998 by Daniel de Rauglaudre, a researcher at Inria, and written in OCaml.

faqs.question: What is Roglo?
faqs.answer: Roglo is a large public genealogy database, containing more than 8.5 million individuals, built on the GeneWeb engine.

faqs.question: Does GeneWeb support GEDCOM?
faqs.answer: Yes, GeneWeb can import and export genealogical data in both the GEDCOM standard and its own native GW format.

faqs.question: What platforms does GeneWeb run on?
faqs.answer: GeneWeb runs on Unix, Linux, Microsoft Windows, and macOS.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host GeneWeb?
faqs.answer: Yes, GeneWeb is self-hosted software; users run it locally for private research or deploy it to a server to make a database accessible online.

enrichment.short_description: GeoRanker is a local SEO and SERP data platform offering rank tracking, citation finding, and a large-scale search results API across Google, Bing, YouTube, Yahoo, Baidu, and Naver.
enrichment.seo_title: GeoRanker Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GeoRanker is a local SEO rank tracking and SERP data API platform. Compare its pricing tiers, features, and top alternatives for local SEO teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GeoRanker is a local SEO and search engine results data platform that positions itself as 'the first SEO Tool Suite addressed to local,' combining a large-scale SERP (search engine results page) API with a suite of rank tracking and local search optimization tools. According to company-tracking sources, GeoRanker was founded in 2011, originally established in Bucharest, Romania, with the company's registered headquarters more recently reported in Luxembourg (specifically Leudelange), reflecting a European base of operations that has evolved over more than a decade of operation. Third-party business intelligence sources, including a 2025 profile from Getlatka, reported that GeoRanker generates approximately $1.2 million in annual revenue with an eleven-person team, indicating the company operates as a lean, self-sustaining, and reportedly unfunded or bootstrapped business (Crunchbase-style aggregator data lists it as an unfunded company) rather than a venture-backed SaaS startup, and the company states it serves a customer base exceeding 20,000 users. GeoRanker's core product is a large-scale SERP data API that retrieves real-time paid and organic search results from more than 50,000 distinct geographic locations worldwide, covering multiple search engines including Google, Bing, Baidu, YouTube, Yahoo, and Naver, across both desktop and mobile result sets. This API-first architecture allows the company to power not only its own end-user SEO tools but also third-party software companies and agencies that need raw, location-specific search data at scale, positioning GeoRanker as both a direct-to-marketer SEO tool and a B2B data infrastructure provider for other SEO software vendors. Layered on top of the raw data API is a full suite of local SEO tools, including local citation finding (identifying and auditing business listings across directories relevant to local search rankings), rank checking and tracking by precise geographic location (critical for multi-location businesses and franchises that need to know how they rank in each specific city or neighborhood rather than just nationally), keyword research and keyword density analysis, competitive intelligence tools for analyzing competitors' rankings and advertising presence, voice search data extraction (tracking how content performs in voice-assistant search results), and a Google Ads competitive audit feature. The company has also been featured in mainstream business and marketing press, including Entrepreneur, Martech, and Forbes, lending third-party visibility to its position in the SEO tools market. GeoRanker's pricing structure is built around a monthly credit allowance rather than flat per-feature pricing, with credit consumption calculated as a function of the number of keywords tracked, the number of locations monitored, device type (desktop and mobile are billed separately, effectively doubling credit use if both are needed), and the number of search engines queried. As of the most recent published pricing, GeoRanker offers three named tiers: PRO at $99 per month, which includes 10,000 monthly credits, up to 50 projects, 100 tracked keywords, and access to mobile rank tracking, custom location tracking, keyword discovery, competition analysis, monitoring, API access, white-label reporting, Google Sheets integration, automated reports, and live support; AGENCIES at $249 per month, which raises the allowance to 50,000 monthly credits, 100 projects, and 500 tracked keywords, and adds advertiser competition analysis and a website ranking tool on top of everything in PRO; and ENTERPRISES at $490 per month, which provides 120,000 monthly credits, 100 projects, and 1,500 tracked keywords with the full feature set unlocked. All plans are offered on a pay-as-you-go, no-contract basis with the ability to cancel at any time, payments accepted via PayPal among other methods, and no setup fees; the company has also referenced a first-month promotional credit allowance for new customers to test the platform before committing to a full monthly plan. Because credits do not roll over between billing periods, customers need to size their plan to their actual monthly usage rather than banking unused capacity, which is a meaningful operational consideration for agencies managing rank tracking across many client accounts and locations. In the competitive SEO tools landscape, GeoRanker overlaps with general-purpose rank trackers and SEO suites such as SEMrush, Ahrefs, Moz, and BrightLocal, but differentiates itself specifically around hyper-local, location-precise rank tracking at scale (tracking rankings from 50,000+ distinct locations) and around offering its underlying SERP data as a raw, resellable API rather than only as a finished, closed dashboard product, which appeals particularly to SEO agencies, franchise and multi-location businesses, and other software companies that need to build local search visibility into their own products. Its long operating history since 2011, more than a decade before many venture-funded SEO tools entered the market, combined with its reported profitability and lean team size, suggests a stable, niche-focused business model built around serving local SEO practitioners and data-hungry SaaS integrators rather than chasing broad, all-in-one SEO suite positioning against category leaders like SEMrush or Ahrefs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GeoRanker, founded in 2011 and now headquartered in Luxembourg after originating in Bucharest, Romania, is a local SEO and SERP data platform built around a large-scale search results API covering Google, Bing, Baidu, YouTube, Yahoo, and Naver across more than 50,000 geographic locations.

Reported as a lean, roughly eleven-person, largely bootstrapped company generating an estimated $1.2 million in annual revenue with over 20,000 customers, GeoRanker serves both direct SEO practitioners and other software companies that need raw local search data via API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform combines local citation finding, precise geographic rank tracking, keyword research and density analysis, competitive intelligence, voice search data extraction, and Google Ads competitive auditing.
A large-scale SERP API underpins the entire product, allowing agencies and SaaS companies to pull raw, location-specific ranking data at scale for their own tools and reports.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: GeoRanker uses a monthly credit system rather than flat feature pricing, with credit usage determined by keywords tracked, locations monitored, device type, and number of search engines queried; credits do not roll over between billing periods.

Three published tiers are available: PRO at $99/month (10,000 credits, 50 projects, 100 keywords), AGENCIES at $249/month (50,000 credits, 100 projects, 500 keywords), and ENTERPRISES at $490/month (120,000 credits, 100 projects, 1,500 keywords), all offered pay-as-you-go with no contracts.

features.description: Identifies and audits business listings across directories relevant to local search ranking factors.

features.description: Tracks keyword rankings by precise location, useful for multi-location businesses and franchises.

features.title: Competitive intelligence
features.description: Analyzes competitors' organic rankings and advertising presence, including advertiser competition analysis.

features.description: Tracks how content performs in voice-assistant search results.

features.description: Agencies can generate white-labeled client reports and integrate raw ranking data via API and Google Sheets integration.

features.title: Automated reporting
features.description: Scheduled, automated reports keep stakeholders updated on ranking performance without manual pulls.
