Raw SERP API access appeals to agencies and SaaS companies building their own tools
Multiple search engines covered beyond just Google, including Bing, Baidu, YouTube, Yahoo, and Naver
No-contract, pay-as-you-go billing with cancel-anytime flexibility
Long operating history since 2011 with a large existing customer base
cons: Credit-based pricing can be complex to estimate, especially when tracking both desktop and mobile
Credits do not roll over between billing periods, so unused capacity is lost
Entry pricing at $99/month is higher than some general-purpose rank trackers' base tiers
Smaller brand recognition compared to category leaders like SEMrush or Ahrefs
Company size and team are lean, which may limit support responsiveness at scale

faqs.question: How does GeoRanker pricing work?
faqs.answer: GeoRanker uses a monthly credit system where usage is calculated based on the number of keywords, locations, device types, and search engines tracked; plans range from $99/month to $490/month.

faqs.question: What search engines does GeoRanker track?
faqs.answer: GeoRanker tracks rankings across Google, Bing, Baidu, YouTube, Yahoo, and Naver, on both desktop and mobile.

faqs.question: Is GeoRanker good for franchises and multi-location businesses?
faqs.answer: Yes, its core strength is precise, location-by-location rank tracking, which is especially useful for businesses that need visibility in specific cities or neighborhoods.

faqs.question: Does GeoRanker offer an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, GeoRanker's core product is a large-scale SERP data API that other agencies and software companies can integrate into their own tools.

faqs.question: When was GeoRanker founded?
faqs.answer: GeoRanker was founded in 2011, originally based in Bucharest, Romania, with its headquarters more recently reported in Luxembourg.

faqs.question: Does GeoRanker require a long-term contract?
faqs.answer: No, GeoRanker is offered on a pay-as-you-go basis with no contracts and the ability to cancel at any time.

use_cases.title: Multi-location and franchise rank tracking
use_cases.description: Businesses with many physical locations track how they rank for relevant keywords in each specific city or neighborhood.

use_cases.title: SEO agency client reporting
use_cases.description: Agencies use white-label reports, automated reporting, and API access to manage ranking data across many client accounts.

use_cases.title: SaaS integration of local search data
use_cases.description: Software companies integrate GeoRanker's SERP API directly into their own products to power location-based search features.

features: API access, white-label reports, Sheets integration

enrichment.short_description: Gerbera is a free, open-source UPnP media server written in C++ that streams music, video, and photos to UPnP and DLNA-compatible devices on a home network.
enrichment.seo_title: Gerbera Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Gerbera is a free, open-source UPnP and DLNA media server for streaming music, video, and photos across your home network. See features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Gerbera is a free, open-source UPnP media server designed to stream digital media, including music, video, and photos, across a home network to any UPnP-compliant device, such as smart TVs, game consoles, and dedicated media players. The project began in 2016 as a fork of MediaTomb, an earlier UPnP media server whose upstream development had stalled; Gerbera's own project description explicitly credits this lineage, describing itself as being 'proudly built upon MediaTomb 0.12.1, continuing that project's great legacy.' The software is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2), with copyright notices spanning from the original MediaTomb developers (2005-2008) through current Gerbera contributors (2016-2025), reflecting nearly two decades of combined development history across both projects. As of the most recent data gathered for this profile, the Gerbera GitHub repository has accumulated 1,400 stars and 223 forks, with 41 tagged releases, the most recent being version 3.2.1 released in March 2026, indicating an actively maintained project with a regular release cadence rather than an abandoned or dormant fork. Technically, Gerbera is written primarily in C++ (accounting for roughly 74.5% of the codebase), with JavaScript making up about 14.5% (used for server-side scripting of import and transcoding logic) and CMake used for its build system. Its core dependencies include libupnp or the alternative libnpupnp library for implementing the UPnP protocol itself, pugixml for XML parsing, jsoncpp for JSON handling, and SQLite3 as its embedded database engine for storing media metadata; optional dependencies extend its capabilities further, including FFmpeg and TagLib for deeper media metadata extraction and thumbnail generation, and the Duktape JavaScript engine for enabling custom, script-based import and transcoding rules. Functionally, Gerbera provides a browser-based Web UI that presents both a tree view of its internal media database and a view of the underlying file system, letting users add, remove, edit, and browse media alongside its extracted metadata. It automatically extracts metadata from a wide range of audio and image formats, including MP3, OGG, AAC, M4A, FLAC, WavPack, and JPG among others, and can generate thumbnails for supported media types. For devices or media formats that cannot be played natively, Gerbera supports flexible transcoding through configurable plugins or external scripts, converting media into a format compatible with the requesting device on the fly. Additional features include automatic rescanning of watched directories so newly added media is detected without manual intervention, Last.fm scrobbling support for tracking listening history, and the ability to include external URLs (such as internet radio streams) as browsable media items alongside local files. Gerbera runs on Linux, BSD, and macOS, and has been compiled for a wide range of processor architectures beyond typical x86 desktops, including ARM and MIPS, which makes it a popular choice for self-hosters running it on low-power hardware such as Raspberry Pi devices, NAS units, or router-class hardware, in addition to full desktop and server Linux installations. It is worth noting, based on the project's own documentation, that Gerbera is technically a UPnP media server rather than a certified DLNA server, though it provides partial DLNA compatibility that works with many, though not necessarily all, DLNA-certified client devices; this distinction matters for users pairing Gerbera with picky smart TVs or DLNA-only hardware, where some troubleshooting or configuration adjustment may be required. Within the self-hosted and home-lab software ecosystem, Gerbera is most directly comparable to other open-source UPnP/DLNA servers such as MiniDLNA (ReadyMedia), Universal Media Server, and Serviio, as well as more full-featured self-hosted media platforms like Plex, Jellyfin, and Emby, which add features such as user accounts, remote access, and richer client apps beyond the core streaming protocol. Gerbera's particular niche is being a lightweight, protocol-focused, highly configurable UPnP server well suited to users who specifically need broad UPnP/DLNA device compatibility (for older smart TVs, UPnP-capable audio receivers, or games consoles) without the heavier resource footprint or account-based architecture of a platform like Plex or Jellyfin, and its packaging for a wide range of CPU architectures makes it a common choice for router firmware distributions and low-power NAS appliances specifically because of that lightweight footprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Gerbera is a free, open-source UPnP media server that began in 2016 as a continuation of the stalled MediaTomb project. Released under the GPLv2 license, it streams music, video, and photos across a home network to UPnP-compliant devices such as smart TVs and media players.

The project is actively maintained on GitHub with 1,400 stars, 223 forks, and 41 releases, the latest being version 3.2.1, reflecting a steady, community-driven development cadence since its fork from MediaTomb.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Gerbera offers a browser-based Web UI for browsing the media database and file system, automatic metadata extraction from formats like MP3, OGG, FLAC, AAC, and JPG, thumbnail generation, and flexible plugin- or script-based transcoding for incompatible devices.
Additional capabilities include automatic directory rescanning, Last.fm scrobbling, and support for external URLs such as internet radio streams alongside local media.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Gerbera is completely free and open source under the GPLv2 license, with no paid tiers or licensing fees.
Users self-host Gerbera on their own hardware, from Raspberry Pi devices and NAS units to full desktop or server installations on Linux, BSD, or macOS.

features.title: Browser-based Web UI
features.description: Provides a tree view of both the media database and the underlying file system for adding, removing, editing, and browsing media.

features.title: Automatic metadata extraction
features.description: Extracts metadata from MP3, OGG, AAC, M4A, FLAC, WavPack, JPG, and many other audio and image formats.

features.title: Thumbnail generation
features.description: Automatically generates thumbnails for supported media types for easier browsing on client devices.

features.title: Flexible transcoding
features.description: Converts media formats on the fly using configurable plugins or external scripts for devices that cannot play the original format natively.

features.title: Automatic directory rescans
features.description: Detects newly added media in watched directories without requiring manual database refreshes.

features.title: Last.fm scrobbling
features.description: Tracks and reports listening history to Last.fm directly from the media server.

features.title: External URL support
features.description: Includes internet radio streams and other external URLs as browsable media items alongside local files.

features.title: Broad platform and architecture support
features.description: Runs on Linux, BSD, and macOS across x86, ARM, and MIPS architectures, including low-power devices like Raspberry Pi and NAS units.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPLv2
Lightweight footprint suitable for low-power hardware like Raspberry Pi and NAS devices
Broad format support with automatic metadata extraction and thumbnail generation
Flexible, scriptable transcoding for compatibility with a wide range of UPnP devices
Actively maintained with regular releases since its 2016 fork from MediaTomb
cons: Not a certified DLNA server, only partial DLNA compatibility, which can cause issues with some devices
Requires self-hosting and configuration knowledge, unlike consumer-friendly platforms like Plex
No built-in user accounts, remote access, or mobile apps found in fuller platforms like Jellyfin or Plex
Web UI is more utilitarian than the polished client apps offered by commercial alternatives
No official support channel beyond community GitHub issues and documentation

faqs.question: Is Gerbera free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gerbera is free and open source, released under the GPLv2 license.

faqs.question: Is Gerbera the same as MediaTomb?
faqs.answer: No, but it is directly descended from it. Gerbera began in 2016 as a fork of MediaTomb after that project's development stalled.

faqs.question: Does Gerbera support DLNA?
faqs.answer: Gerbera is a UPnP media server with partial DLNA compatibility; it works with many DLNA devices but is not a certified DLNA server.

faqs.question: What platforms does Gerbera run on?
faqs.answer: Gerbera runs on Linux, BSD, and macOS across x86, ARM, and MIPS architectures, including low-power devices like Raspberry Pi.

faqs.question: Can Gerbera transcode media for incompatible devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gerbera supports flexible transcoding through configurable plugins or external scripts.

faqs.question: What media formats does Gerbera support?
faqs.answer: Gerbera extracts metadata from and streams formats including MP3, OGG, AAC, M4A, FLAC, WavPack, JPG, and more.

use_cases.title: Home network media streaming
use_cases.description: Stream a personal music, video, or photo library to smart TVs, media players, and other UPnP-compliant devices on a home network.

use_cases.title: Low-power NAS or Raspberry Pi media server
use_cases.description: Deploy a lightweight media server on low-power hardware like a NAS unit or Raspberry Pi without the overhead of a full media platform.

use_cases.title: Legacy device compatibility
use_cases.description: Serve media to older smart TVs, UPnP audio receivers, or game consoles that require standard UPnP/DLNA protocol support.

features: Web UI for media and file system browsing

enrichment.short_description: Leon is a free, open-source, self-hosted personal AI assistant supporting voice and text interaction, agentic task execution, and extensible custom skills.
enrichment.seo_title: Leon AI (getleon.ai) Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Leon is a free, open-source personal AI assistant you can self-host. Explore its voice/text features, skills system, architecture, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Leon, marketed at getleon.ai and developed in the open on GitHub under the leon-ai organization, is a free, open-source personal AI assistant designed to run on a user's own server rather than relying on a closed, cloud-only commercial voice assistant like Alexa, Google Assistant, or Siri. The project traces its origin to 2017 and has been actively developed since 2019, created by developer Louis Grenard, known publicly as grenlouis. Released under the permissive MIT License, Leon has grown into one of the more prominent open-source personal assistant projects on GitHub, currently showing approximately 17,400 stars, 1,500 forks, and 276 watchers, a scale of community interest that places it well ahead of most niche self-hosted assistant projects, and the project has attracted institutional sponsorship from OpenAI, JetBrains, and MacStadium, lending it additional credibility and infrastructure support beyond pure volunteer contribution. Leon's core value proposition is built around privacy and local control: it is designed to be practical, privacy-aware, and grounded in a user's real environment, and it can be configured to operate entirely offline, removing dependence on third-party cloud services for its core functionality, which stands in contrast to mainstream voice assistants that route most or all processing through vendor cloud infrastructure. At the same time, Leon is flexible enough to integrate with both local and remote AI providers when a user does want to leverage larger cloud-hosted language models for more capable reasoning, giving users a choice between maximum privacy and maximum capability depending on their needs. Functionally, Leon supports both voice and text-based interaction: users can speak to Leon and receive spoken responses using integrated text-to-speech and speech-to-text engines, which can be configured to use either cloud-based providers or fully offline, on-device solutions, or interact via a more conventional text chat interface when voice isn't convenient. As of the most recent documentation reviewed, Leon operates through multiple distinct execution modes: a 'smart' mode that automatically selects the appropriate response strategy, a 'controlled' mode that executes deterministic, purpose-built native skills for reliable, repeatable tasks, and an 'agent' mode that performs step-by-step planning and execution for more open-ended, multi-step requests, reflecting a broader shift in the project toward agentic, tool-using AI architecture rather than simple command-and-response scripting. This is reinforced by the project's own description of itself as being 'built around tools, context, memory, and agentic execution,' and by its layered memory system, which separately tracks user preferences, ongoing daily context, and historical discussion, allowing Leon to behave more like a persistent personal assistant than a stateless chatbot. Leon's extensibility comes from its skills architecture, split into native skills (built directly into the core system for speed and reliability) and agent skills (more flexible, tool-based capabilities that can be composed dynamically), both of which developers can create and share with the broader community, and the project explicitly documents Node.js and Python bridge layers with toolkit definitions, along with Python services communicating over a TCP server, showing a deliberately polyglot architecture that lets contributors build skills in whichever language best suits the task. On the technology stack itself, Leon's codebase is roughly 68% TypeScript, 18% Python, and 10% JavaScript, with additional Sass/SCSS and HTML for its web application interface, and it requires a self-hosted server environment with Node.js 16 or higher, npm 8 or higher, Python 3.9.10, and the Pipenv dependency manager, with installation options via a CLI-based setup wizard, Docker containerization, or a fully manual install process, and support across Linux, macOS, and Windows. At the time of research, the project is undergoing a significant architectural transition, with its GitHub materials describing a '2.0 Developer Preview' phase and a version number in the 1.0.0-beta range on its stable branch, indicating Leon is still evolving rapidly toward a more mature, agent-centric rewrite rather than being a finished, static product; prospective users should expect ongoing breaking changes and active development rather than a fully polished, versioned 1.0 release. Because Leon is a community open-source project rather than a company, there is no publicly disclosed company size, headquarters, or revenue; support and roadmap direction happen through GitHub issues, community discussion, and the project's own documentation site at docs.getleon.ai. In the broader competitive landscape, Leon sits alongside other open-source or privacy-focused personal assistant and local-AI projects such as Mycroft (a now largely dormant open voice assistant), Home Assistant's Assist voice pipeline, and a growing wave of self-hosted LLM agent frameworks, while differentiating itself specifically through its long project history dating back to 2017, its dedicated voice-plus-text personal assistant framing (as opposed to a general-purpose home automation platform or a bare LLM orchestration framework), and its explicit agentic execution model that lets it plan and carry out multi-step tasks using tools rather than only answering single-turn questions, positioning Leon as a serious, privacy-respecting alternative for developers and self-hosting enthusiasts who want a customizable personal AI assistant that they fully own and control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Leon (getleon.ai) is a free, open-source, self-hosted personal AI assistant created by developer Louis Grenard, with roots dating to 2017 and active development since 2019. Released under the MIT License, it supports both voice and text interaction and can run fully offline for maximum privacy.

The project has grown to roughly 17,400 GitHub stars and 1,500 forks, with sponsorship from OpenAI, JetBrains, and MacStadium, and is currently undergoing a major architectural evolution toward agentic, tool-based execution in its '2.0 Developer Preview' phase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Leon supports three execution modes: 'smart' (automatic strategy selection), 'controlled' (deterministic native skills), and 'agent' (step-by-step planning for multi-step tasks), backed by a layered memory system for preferences, daily context, and discussion history.

Its extensible skills architecture splits into native skills (fast, built-in) and agent skills (flexible, tool-based), with Node.js and Python bridge layers letting developers build and share custom capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Leon is completely free and open source under the MIT License, with no paid tiers or subscription fees for the software itself.

Users self-host Leon on their own server (Linux, macOS, or Windows), so the only costs involved are the user's own infrastructure and, optionally, any third-party cloud AI provider fees if configured to use remote models.

features.title: Voice and text interaction
features.description: Supports both spoken conversation via integrated text-to-speech/speech-to-text and conventional text-based chat interaction.

features.title: Offline-capable, privacy-focused design
features.description: Can be configured to run entirely offline, removing dependence on third-party cloud services for core functionality.

features.title: Multiple execution modes
features.description: Offers 'smart,' 'controlled,' and 'agent' modes for automatic strategy selection, deterministic skills, or multi-step agentic planning.

features.title: Layered memory system
features.description: Tracks user preferences, ongoing daily context, and historical discussion separately for more persistent, personalized behavior.

features.title: Extensible skills architecture
features.description: Supports native skills (built-in, fast) and agent skills (flexible, tool-based) that developers can create and share.

features.title: Local and remote AI provider support
features.description: Can integrate with local, on-device AI models or remote cloud-hosted providers depending on the user's privacy and capability needs.

features.title: Cross-platform self-hosting
features.description: Runs on Linux, macOS, and Windows via CLI setup, Docker, or manual installation.

features.title: Polyglot bridge architecture
features.description: Node.js and Python bridge layers with toolkit definitions allow skills to be built in the language best suited to the task.

pros: Completely free and open source under the MIT License
Can run fully offline for strong privacy and no dependency on third-party cloud services
Long project history since 2017 with a large, active GitHub community (~17,400 stars)
Backed by notable sponsors including OpenAI, JetBrains, and MacStadium
Flexible agentic execution model for multi-step task planning, not just single-turn commands
cons: Requires self-hosting and a nontrivial technical setup (Node.js, Python, Pipenv dependencies)
Currently mid-transition to a '2.0 Developer Preview' architecture, meaning ongoing breaking changes
No official commercial support; relies on community documentation and GitHub issues
Voice recognition and TTS quality depend on which providers are configured, offline options may be less accurate than top commercial cloud services
Not a plug-and-play consumer product like Alexa or Google Assistant

faqs.question: Is Leon (getleon.ai) free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Leon is completely free and open source under the MIT License.

faqs.question: Who created Leon?
faqs.answer: Leon was created by developer Louis Grenard, with the project dating back to 2017 and active development since 2019.

faqs.question: Can Leon run fully offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, Leon can be configured to operate entirely offline, without relying on third-party cloud services, for maximum privacy.

faqs.question: What platforms does Leon support?
faqs.answer: Leon runs on Linux, macOS, and Windows, and can be installed via a CLI setup wizard, Docker, or manual installation.

faqs.question: Does Leon support voice interaction?
faqs.answer: Yes, Leon supports both voice (via text-to-speech and speech-to-text) and text-based chat interaction.

faqs.question: Is Leon still in active development?
faqs.answer: Yes, Leon is undergoing a major architectural transition and is currently in a '2.0 Developer Preview' phase with ongoing active development.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused personal assistant
use_cases.description: Users who don't want their voice assistant data sent to third-party clouds self-host Leon to run assistant functionality entirely offline.

use_cases.title: Custom skill development
use_cases.description: Developers build and share custom native or agent skills to extend Leon's capabilities for specific personal or home automation tasks.

use_cases.title: Multi-step agentic task automation
use_cases.description: Users leverage Leon's 'agent' execution mode to plan and carry out multi-step tasks rather than issuing single, isolated commands.

features: Smart, controlled, and agent execution modes
Extensible native and agent skills architecture
